message:
br-sq7ae: snapshot atfork handlers before invoking user callbacks

run_atfork_prepare/parent/child held ATFORK_HANDLERS.lock() across
arbitrary user-callback execution. A handler that transitively called
pthread_atfork re-locked the non-recursive std::sync::Mutex on the same
thread and deadlocked. Fix: clone the Vec<AtforkHandlers> under the
guard, drop the guard, then iterate the snapshot.

Adds a regression test in tests/pthread_abi_test.rs that registers a
prepare handler which calls pthread_atfork from inside its callback;
before the fix this test hangs, after it observes the new entry.

Classified by /deadlock-finder-and-fixer as Class 1 (classic mutex
self-deadlock via foreign-code callback). Passes the False-Positive
Rule: a concrete interleaving exists (library lazy-init from a prepare
handler is a common pattern).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-36hiy","title":"[MEDIUM] fuzz: stateful pidfd and timer differential harness","description":"[MEDIUM] fuzz: add stateful pidfd/timer differential harness

Add a stateful differential fuzz target in `crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/` for syscall wrappers whose behavior depends on handle lifecycle and sequencing.

Recommended syscall set:
- `pidfd_open` (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:5055`)
- `pidfd_send_signal` (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:5067`)
- `timer_settime` (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:7569`)
- `timer_gettime` (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:7592`)

Fuzz shape:
- generate short action sequences instead of single calls
- model create/use/close/delete/reuse transitions
- compare FrankenLibC vs host libc on errno, return values, and stale-handle behavior
- bias toward null pointers, deleted handles, invalid signal numbers, and mixed valid/invalid sequencing

Why now:
- recent backlog work added many focused parity tests for deleted handles and invalid inputs, but sequence-sensitive regressions will hide between those single-case pins.","notes":"Closed by cc-libc (JSONL edit — same br CLI lookup bug).

Delivered crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_pidfd_timer.rs as a stateful harness with a small HandleTable tracking pidfds and timer ids and their Live/Stale state.

Ops: PidfdOpen/Send/Close; TimerCreate/Settime/Gettime/Getoverrun/Delete. Each op picks a slot in the handle table (modulo) so sequences like create -> close -> reuse exercise stale-handle behavior.

Oracles:
1. Return-code contract: 0 / documented non-negative / -1 only.
2. Lifecycle invariant: once a pidfd is closed or a timer is deleted, any subsequent operation on that id must return -1. The harness asserts this directly after every op that touches a Stale slot.
3. Canonical timespec: timer_gettime success result must have nsec in 0..1_000_000_000.

Safety: pidfds target only {0, own pid, huge-nonexistent, -1}. Signals sent via pidfd_send_signal are restricted to {0, SIGWINCH, SIGURG} — all safe to receive in the fuzz binary. A global LOCK serializes iterations; a cleanup() pass closes live pidfds and deletes live timers before returning from the op loop.

Deliberately not included (future work):
- libc-symbol differential (extern "C" binds to only one impl; needs dlsym plumbing).
- Real signal-delivery observation via sigaction.

Wired as `fuzz_pidfd_timer` bin. Validation: cargo build + cargo clippy --no-deps -- -D warnings both clean.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc-libc","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:31:05.257917015Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:35.884103Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:35.884103Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["differential","fuzz","stateful","syscall"]}

 {"id":"bd-gcy","title":"bd-c1x subtask: Futex condvar core implementation (wait/signal/broadcast/timedwait)","description":"Background:
- Condvar implementation must coordinate with mutex ownership and futex wake ordering safely.

Goal:
- Implement futex-based condvar core with deterministic sequence and wake semantics.

Deliverables:
1) Internal condvar sequence/counter design.
2) wait path with unlock/relock choreography around futex_wait.
3) signal/broadcast wake policy.
4) Timed wait with absolute deadline handling.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Core condvar operations satisfy spec for success/error/timeout cases.
- No missed wakeups in deterministic stress tests.

Verification & Logging:
- Unit tests: signal-before-wait, wait-before-signal, broadcast-many, timeout races.
- Structured logs: trace_id, mode, condvar_state, mutex_state, wake_count, timeout_state, errno.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleHawk","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:00:51.531693883Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:32.207008001Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:32.206927771Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["critique","futex","implementation","pthread","testing"],"comments":[{"id":1088,"issue_id":"bd-gcy","author":"PurpleHawk","text":"Completed futex condvar core implementation (bd-gcy). Deliverables:

1) CondvarData repr(C) struct with seq counter, waiter count, associated mutex, and clock_id — overlays onto pthread_cond_t memory (20 bytes, fits in 48-byte struct).

2) condvar_wait(): atomic unlock-wait-relock choreography using sys_futex. Captures seq before unlock, parks with FUTEX_WAIT on seq word, relocks via CAS+futex loop on return. Validates mutex association invariant (EINVAL on mismatch).

3) condvar_timedwait(): uses FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET with absolute timeout. FUTEX_CLOCK_REALTIME flag selects clock domain matching condvar's configured clock. Returns ETIMEDOUT on deadline expiry after relocking mutex (per POSIX).

4) condvar_signal(): increments seq + futex_wake(1). condvar_broadcast(): increments seq + futex_wake(INT_MAX). Both are no-ops when no waiters (seq still incremented for consistency).

5) relock_mutex() helper: simple CAS loop with futex parking for relocking after condvar wait.

6) 64 total tests pass: 41 contract tests (bd-blg) + 23 core implementation tests including 4 threaded integration tests:
   - core_condvar_wait_signal_roundtrip (single waiter + signal)
   - core_condvar_broadcast_wakes_all (4 waiters + broadcast)
   - core_condvar_timedwait_expires (past deadline -> ETIMEDOUT)
   - core_condvar_mutex_association_mismatch (EINVAL on different mutex)

7) Exports from pthread module: CondvarData, condvar_init/destroy/wait/timedwait/signal/broadcast.

Verification: cargo test -p frankenlibc-core -- pthread::cond (64 passed, 0 failed). cargo check --all-targets clean.","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:26Z"}]}

-{"id": "bd-ie8zc", "title": "atoi clamps to INT_MAX on overflow instead of truncating per POSIX (int)strtol equivalence", "description": "## Defect

\`crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdlib/conversion.rs\` atoi() clamps
the strtol result into i32 range:

    if val > i32::MAX as i64 { i32::MAX }
    else if val < i32::MIN as i64 { i32::MIN }
    else { val as i32 }

POSIX SUSv4 specifies that atoi(str) is equivalent to
\`(int) strtol(str, (char **)NULL, 10)\`. \`(int) long_val\` on
2's-complement machines (all targets we support) is a TRUNCATION
cast, not a saturating clamp.

Observed divergence (found by fuzz_stdlib):
- Input: \"99999999999999\" (fourteen 9s)
- strtol → 99999999999999 (fits in i64, no overflow, errno unchanged)
- glibc atoi: (int) 99999999999999 = 0x107A3FFF = 276447231
- Our atoi: clamps, returns INT_MAX = 2147483647

## Fix

Replace the if-else clamp with a bare \`val as i32\` cast. C
standard says overflow behavior is UB; POSIX equivalence with
(int)strtol pins it down to truncation on 2's-complement.
matches glibc's observable behavior.

## Severity

[MEDIUM] (P2). Any C program that relies on atoi's documented
POSIX equivalence to \`(int)strtol\` gets a different value from
us than from glibc when the source string overflows i32. Likely
rare in production (most callers already bound their input), but
a documented divergence from glibc.", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "bug", "created_at": "2026-04-23T17:01:52.755209221Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-23T17:14:00.000000000Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "closed_at": "2026-04-23T17:14:00.000000000Z", "closed_by": "cc-libc", "notes": "

-{"id": "bd-j19j6", "title": "aarch64 raw syscall + clone-tail-args code paths have no CI cross-compile check", "description": "## Defect

frankenlibc-core/src/syscall/raw.rs declares 10 aarch64 syscall0..
syscall6 blocks via #[cfg(target_arch = \"aarch64\")] and a separate
current_clone_tail_args variant for aarch64's different clone
argument order. frankenlibc-core/src/lib.rs and frankenlibc-abi/
src/lib.rs both gate compilation on cfg(any(x86_64, aarch64)), and
multiple closed beads (bd-10pq, bd-h5x) point to aarch64 as
'supported' / 'active bring-up'.

Reality check: there is no CI job, no cross-compile test, and no
deterministic gate script that actually compiles ANY crate for
aarch64. `rg --files | xargs -I _ rg 'aarch64'` only hits the
source-level cfg attrs — no workflow, no cross-build in scripts/,
no triple override in Cargo metadata.

If any of the 10 aarch64 syscall blocks has a typo or wrong
register constraint, we'd only notice when someone first tries
to build on an actual aarch64 host. Meanwhile, the bead closures
overstate current support.

## Fix

Add one of:
1. `scripts/check_aarch64_crosscompile.sh` that runs `cargo check
   --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu -p frankenlibc-core -p
   frankenlibc-abi` with the cross toolchain, as a deterministic
   gate that can run in CI.
2. A tests/conformance/ fixture that exercises clone-tail-args
   layout via a type-level compile check.

Option 1 is the minimal-scope fix — cross-compile check catches
register-constraint typos and missing cfg gates without needing
an aarch64 runner.

## Severity

target and all known consumers use it. But the closure notes

so the first aarch64 consumer will hit avoidable breakage.", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "bug", "created_at": "2026-04-23T16:56:50.661732236Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-23T17:15:00.000000000Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "closed_at": "2026-04-23T17:15:00.000000000Z", "closed_by": "cc-libc", "notes": "Fixed in prior commit: scripts/check_aarch64_crosscompile.sh gates cargo check --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu for frankenlibc-core + frankenlibc-abi.  {"id":"bd-sttk9","title":"fuzz: read/write/pread/pwrite buffer + count validation","description":"## Scope
Add crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_read_write.rs targeting frankenlibc_abi::unistd_abi::{read,write,pread,pwrite,readv,writev}. Focus on buffer-bound validation + short-read/short-write semantics + errno correctness for invalid fd/len/buf.

## Archetype
1 + 3: Crash-detector + **differential** against libc for matched calls, using a seeded pipe pair and /dev/zero / /dev/null fds as deterministic backends.

## Inputs
struct ReadWriteFuzzInput {
  op: u8,                 // 0=read, 1=write, 2=pread, 3=pwrite, 4=readv, 5=writev
  fd_selector: u8,        // 0=/dev/zero, 1=/dev/null, 2=pipe-read-end, 3=pipe-write-end, 4=closed-fd, 5=-1
  buf_len: u16,           // 0..=32768
  buf_offset: u8,         // shift within a larger allocation (exercises decide's address checks)
  poison_ptr: bool,       // NULL or address we can catch
  offset: i64,            // for pread/pwrite
  iovec_count: u8,        // 0..=8 for readv/writev
}

## Invariants
- No panic on any input
- rc is a non-negative byte count OR -1 with errno in {EBADF, EFAULT, EINVAL, EIO, EAGAIN, EINTR, EPIPE, ENOSPC, ESPIPE, EOVERFLOW}
- Host parity for the /dev/zero, /dev/null, closed-fd cases: ours matches libc::read/write bit-for-bit
- readv/writev total byte count equals the sum of per-iovec byte counts
- Buffer is not read/written past requested len (ASAN + explicit sentinel check before and after)

## Expected bug classes
- Off-by-one in iovec loop, iov_len overflow, fd validation bypass, membrane decision mismatches for cross-page buffers, missing EINTR retry, sign handling for large counts (SSIZE_MAX), ESPIPE not mapped for pread/pwrite on pipes

## Sentinel pattern
Allocate 2*buf_len + 2 pages, place guard bytes (0xAA) before and after the requested window; after the call assert guard bytes unchanged.

## Score
5 × 5 × /2 ≈ 12.5 — tied with bd-o4yda for priority.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:39:39.154205881Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:21:22.578845312Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:21:22.576538966Z","close_reason":"fuzz_read_write harness committed and green: 16.3M execs / 108K exec/s in 151s. Sentinel-byte guards before/after every buffer window catch any over-count write. Differential parity against libc for non-pipe backends. Two harness-side bugs fixed during bring-up (count > buf_size overrun, buf_offset into post-pad). Zero real impl bugs under the invariant set — the read/write wrapper is solid.  {"id":"bd-xpgak","title":"xdr_pointer / xdr_reference: no depth limit on caller-supplied recursive procs","description":"## Defect

`xdr_pointer` (rpc_abi.rs:1399) and `xdr_reference` (rpc_abi.rs:1362)
take a caller-supplied per-element xdr_proc. If the caller passes a
proc that recursively invokes xdr_pointer on a nested field (e.g.,
a generated stub for an XDR type with a self-referential
optional pointer like `struct tree { tree *left; tree *right;}`),
attacker-supplied wire data of repeated `more=1` flags walks the
recursion arbitrarily deep — same DoS class as bd-lc5ss but
caller-controlled rather than library-internal.

bd-lc5ss fixed the library's own internal recursive type
(xdr_pmaplist) by rewriting iteratively. That fix doesn't help
caller-supplied recursive types; the library has no per-thread
depth tracker on xdr_pointer/xdr_reference at all.

## Fix

Add a `XDR_RECURSE_DEPTH` thread_local Cell<usize> with a hard cap
(e.g. 256). xdr_reference increments on entry, decrements on exit,
and returns XDR_FALSE if the counter exceeds the cap. This is
defense-in-depth: callers using rpcgen-generated stubs with
sensible types never approach the cap, but a malicious wire stream
fed to a vulnerable caller is bounded by the depth limit instead
of by available stack.

## Severity

[MEDIUM] (P3). The library-internal recursive type was fixed by
bd-lc5ss; this remaining attack surface requires a vulnerable
CALLER (one that passes a recursive proc), so it's a smaller blast
radius than bd-lc5ss but still a real wire-controlled DoS for any
RPC server that exposes recursive XDR types.","notes":"Fixed in 9d326b98: XDR_REFERENCE_DEPTH thread_local Cell<u32> with hard cap of 256. xdr_reference increments on entry, decrements on exit. Added regression test xdr_reference_depth_limit_refuses_deep_recursive_wire_without_overflow with 1024-deep recursive RecursiveNode chain. 90/90 xdr_abi_test pass; clippy clean.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T02:00:51.865514841Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T03:53:00Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T03:53:00Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id": "bd-xx2j1", "title": "printf %n in hardened mode denies writes to untracked pointers (stack, external heap) breaking POSIX contract", "description": "## Defect

\`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdio_abi.rs:3142-3157\` in the %n
path:

    if mode.heals_enabled() || matches!(decision.action, Repair(_)) {
        if let Some(rem) = crate::malloc_abi::known_remaining(ptr_val) {
            if rem < size {
                should_write = false;
                ...
            }
        } else {
            // Pointer not tracked by our allocator.
            should_write = false;      ← BUG
            ...
        }
    }

When \`known_remaining(ptr_val)\` returns \`None\` (pointer isn't in
our tracked allocator — typical for a stack var like
\`let mut count: i32\` in caller code, or an external malloc),
the heal logic sets \`should_write = false\` unconditionally.

Result: in hardened mode, every %n call with a caller-provided
\`int*\` that doesn't happen to land in our tracked heap is
silently skipped. Callers see their int unchanged instead of
the byte-count-so-far that POSIX mandates.

Reproduced by fuzz_printf's fuzz_percent_n_abi:
- Format \"abc%n:%s\" with stack-allocated count
- Expected: count == 3 (bytes \"abc\" written before %n)
- Observed: count == -1 (harness init value, never overwritten)

## Fix

Absence of allocator tracking is not evidence of danger. The
membrane's \`decide()\` already ran; if it returned non-Deny, the
pointer should be writable. Only set \`should_write = false\` in
the inner branch where \`rem < size\` is explicit.

    if let Some(rem) = crate::malloc_abi::known_remaining(ptr_val) {
        if rem < size { should_write = false; ... }
    }
    // else: untracked allocation, rely on decide() verdict

## Severity

[MEDIUM] (P2). %n is security-sensitive (CVE-classic format string
vector) and most production callers don't use it on attacker-
controlled format strings. But programs that legitimately use %n
(e.g., parsing APIs that need consumed-byte count) get wrong
results under hardened mode.", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "bug", "created_at": "2026-04-23T17:07:40.665097128Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-23T17:14:00.000000000Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "closed_at": "2026-04-23T17:14:00.000000000Z", "closed_by": "cc-libc", "notes": "

Fixed in 6d29fb13: removed the else-branch in the heal-path that set should_write=false when known_remaining returned None. Untracked allocations (stack, external heap) now honor the membrane's non-Deny verdict. POSIX %n contract holds under hardened mode."}

 {"id":"bd-xxyb","title":"Investigate Python startup perf regression in LD_PRELOAD smoke suite (51/7/6)","description":"## Problem
LD_PRELOAD smoke suite regressed from 58/0/6 to 51/7/6. All 7 failures are Python-related with failure_signature=startup_perf_regression. Correctness is fine (parity_pass=true) — only startup latency exceeds the perf gate threshold.

## Failing Cases
- strict: python3_print, stress_python_{1..5}
- hardened: stress_python_5

## Probable Cause
Recent changes to signal handling and malloc that added overhead:
1. signal_abi.rs: Signal trampoline installation, SA_RESTORER marshalling, deferred delivery critical sections
2. malloc_abi.rs: Signal critical section entry/exit around every malloc/calloc/free/realloc call
3. host_resolve.rs: Streaming /proc/self/maps parser changes

Python startup is particularly sensitive because it calls sigaction dozens of times during interpreter init, and does heavy allocation.

## Investigation Steps
1. Profile Python startup under LD_PRELOAD to identify the hotspot
2. Check if the malloc signal critical section is the bottleneck (atomic ops on every alloc)
3. Check if sigaction trampoline installation overhead is significant
4. Consider: make signal critical sections opt-in (only when FRANKENLIBC_MODE=hardened)

## Acceptance Criteria
Smoke suite returns to 58/0/6 (or 57/0/7 with the new skipped binary).
